channel.title: Mohit Ranka
channel.description: Engineering leader. Writing on data platforms, developer tooling, reliability, and leadership.

item.title: The line manager role is disappearing faster than you think
item.description: The line manager role is disappearing faster than most career plans assume. Meta has already moved toward one senior manager for roughly every fifty developers. That is not a distant forecast. It is an operating choice already in the market. If you lead engineers — or want to — the useful question …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: Near-real-time is a product promise, not a Kafka cluster
item.description: “Near-real-time” is often used as a synonym for “we bought a streaming stack.” On GTM data platforms, that confusion is expensive. The product promise is about whether a decision-maker can trust a number in time — not whether an event log is busy. At LinkedIn, the Enterprise Data Platform (EDP) was …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: The platform team’s real job is interfaces
item.description: At LinkedIn, the Enterprise Data Platform (EDP) was meant to be the centralized way GTM teams managed and consumed datasets. On paper, that is a clear platform charter. In practice, a platform is only real when its interfaces get adopted — including by teams that already have a path that “works …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: When I still choose a relational database
item.description: In 2013 I wrote RDBMS vs. NOSQL? as a pushback against fashion. The fashion changed costumes — document stores, wide-column, NewSQL, "Postgres is fine," "everything in the lakehouse" — but the underlying mistake did not: picking a datastore from a blog post instead of from access patterns and failure modes.
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: Freshness SLOs: the metric product teams actually feel
item.description: API latency SLOs changed how we run services. GTM data work taught me the sibling idea the hard way: product teams do not experience your job-success chart. They experience a dashboard that is late, wrong, or disagrees with another “official” number. At LinkedIn, BI teams on Power BI and Tableau …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: Saying no as a platform EM without becoming the villain
item.description: Platform engineering managers do not run out of good ideas. They run out of capacity to say yes to every reasonable request without wrecking the shared system. The skill is not blunt refusal. It is no with a path — specific enough that partners can actually execute, firm enough that your …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: Identity systems fail socially before they fail cryptographically
item.description: When people talk about identity and SSO, they reach for algorithms: token lifetimes, key rotation, SAML vs OIDC, session fixation. Those details matter. In systems I have built and operated, the outages and near-misses that hurt most started earlier — as social and product failures wearing security clothing.
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: What “led the web launch” taught me about constraints
item.description: At Postman, “put the product on the web” was not a greenfield rewrite. It was a constraint problem: a desktop-native API tool used by millions of developers, enterprise pressure for browser access, browser security that blocked the old execution model, and a conference date that would not move. I was …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: Incubating 0→1 beside a mature product
item.description: When I was at Postman, the core product was already the default API client for a huge HTTP/HTTPS world — on the order of tens of millions of developers on desktop. That success created a sharp problem: how do you explore what comes after HTTP without slowing the product everyone …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: How I review a distributed design in 45 minutes
item.description: The most expensive design reviews I have run were not missing a box on a diagram. They were missing a decision. One of them froze delivery on an EDP self-serve portal for about a month while two strong engineers disagreed in public about monolith versus microservices — and ownership quietly collapsed …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: Introducing on-call without burning the team
item.description: At Postman, my team owned a large-scale platform surface used by millions of developers. What we did not own — formally — was a predictable operational response. Production issues and public GitHub noise were handled ad hoc. Someone jumped in, or everyone hesitated. Retrospectives lacked a clear accountable role. The system worked …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: GTM datasets need data contracts
item.description: When a go-to-market dashboard is wrong, nobody says “the warehouse is eventually consistent.” They say the number is wrong — and they stop trusting the platform. On LinkedIn’s Enterprise Data Platform (EDP) work, the failure mode was rarely a missing chart type. It was informal truth: datasets without clear producers …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka

item.title: RDBMS vs. NOSQL?
item.description: From our own experience designing and operating a highly available, highly scalable ecommerce platform, we have come to realize that relational databases should only be used when an application really needs the complex query, table join and transaction capabilities of a full-blown relational database. In all other cases, when such …
item.dc:creator: Mohit Ranka
